title: Juice on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; artificial-dye juices take more rounds
stainChemistry: Juice color comes from one of three pigment classes: (1) natural anthocyanins (grape, cranberry, pomegranate, cherry — red-purple-blue), (2) natural carotenoids (orange, mango, carrot, pineapple — yellow-orange), or (3) ADDED synthetic dye — Red 40, Blue 1, Yellow 5 — engineered into almost all kid juice boxes, sports drinks, Gatorade, Kool-Aid, and flavored lemonades. Plus juice always has a lot of SUGAR (10–15g per cup typical), which creates a sticky matrix that caramelizes as it dries and attracts dirt for weeks if not removed. Natural juice responds to standard tannin chemistry. Artificial-dye juice is engineered to resist water (so the color stays in the drink) and often needs 2–3× more cycles plus UV assist (sunlight) to break down the dye. The sugar step is often skipped and is the reason "clean" juice spots re-collect dirt.
whyThisWorks: Cold-water flush first removes sugar before chemistry starts (sugar left in the pad attracts future dirt). Dawn + 3% peroxide oxidizes the natural anthocyanin/carotenoid pigments and gradually breaks down artificial Red 40. White vinegar rinse keeps anthocyanins in their lighter (red) form rather than drifting to dark blue-purple. Weighted dry towels overnight prevent sugar + pigment from wicking back up as a ring. Sunlight is a legit final pass for stubborn artificial-dye cases — Red 40 is UV-degradable.
defaultAssumption: Kid on the rug with a juice box or sippy cup. Fresh + natural juice (orange, apple) = easy. Fresh + Gatorade / Kool-Aid / sports drink = 2–3 rounds plus sunlight. Dried-in juice with sugar matrix = harder; the sugar has caramelized and the pigment is cross-linked.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Good light, juice label if available
spottingProtocol.instruction: Natural anthocyanin (grape, cranberry, pomegranate) = standard tannin protocol. Natural carotenoid (orange, carrot) = similar, sometimes UV-sensitive. Artificial-dye (Red 40 / Blue 1 / Yellow 5 — kid juice boxes, sports drinks, Kool-Aid, Gatorade, flavored lemonade) = engineered to be water-resistant, budget 2–3× the cycles + sunlight finish.

spottingProtocol.technique: Absorb bulk before it reaches the pad
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press white towels straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in. Don't rub — rubbing spreads color and drives sugar deeper.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold Water Flush + Blot — Clear Sugar First
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pour a small amount of cold water on the spot. Blot with dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. You're flushing SUGAR out first — sugar left in the fiber caramelizes and attracts dirt for weeks. Cold only. Don't flood — pad wicking is the enemy.
Dab the Dawn + peroxide mix (step 5) on a hidden carpet area for 2 min. Color transfer to the cloth = carpet dye is lifting; skip peroxide. No transfer = proceed.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp Dawn + 1 cup 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix 1 tbsp Dawn + 1 cup 3% H₂O₂. Dip white cloth, wring damp, press on spot, dwell 5–10 min. Blot. Natural juice: 1–2 cycles. Red 40 / Blue 1 artificial: 2–3 cycles.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia / bleach / alkaline cleaners.

spottingProtocol.technique: Neutralize peroxide + keep anthocyanin on the red side
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp white vinegar + 1 cup cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute vinegar on damp cloth. Press 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide + keeps anthocyanin in its lighter (acidic) color rather than drifting toward blue-purple. Blot dry.
Stack paper towels, weight with a heavy book, leave overnight. Pulls residual sugar + pigment out of the pad before wick-back. Especially important on juice because of sugar content.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Open blinds / daylight on the spot
spottingProtocol.instruction: If a pale artificial-dye ghost remains after two full cycles, open the blinds so sunlight falls on the spot for several hours. UV gradually breaks down Red 40 and Blue 1. This is a legit finishing pass for stubborn dye-spill cases, not a myth.

steps: 1. Identify — natural pigment vs artificial dye.
3. Cold water flush + blot — sugar FIRST.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — heat sets sugar + pigment
warnings: NEVER alkaline cleaners on juice — locks tannin permanently
NEVER hot water — caramelizes sugar, cross-links pigment
Don't skip the sugar flush — sugar left behind attracts future stains
Hidden peroxide test on dyed carpet

homeSolutions.agent: Figure Out If It's Natural Juice or Kid Juice
homeSolutions.instruction: Real 100% natural juice (orange, grape, apple, cranberry) has natural pigment and usually cleans up in 1–2 cycles. KID juice — the small boxes, sports drinks, Gatorade, Kool-Aid, Hi-C, Capri Sun, flavored lemonades — almost always contains synthetic Red 40 or Blue 1 food dye, which is engineered to stay colored in liquid. Plan for 2–3× the work plus a sunlight finish on artificial-dye spills. Both use the same protocol, just different patience.

Press a thick stack of white paper towels STRAIGHT DOWN on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the spill inward to keep the edge contained. Don't rub — rubbing drives juice into the pad and pad contamination is the cause of rings that return.

Juice is a tannin (like wine, tea, coffee) PLUS a lot of sugar. Two rules: (1) NO alkaline cleaners — no baking soda, OxiClean, ammonia, Mr. Clean, most all-purpose sprays — they permanently darken tannin, and (2) SUGAR FLUSH is non-negotiable. If you skip the water flush, sugar stays in the carpet pad, attracts dirt for weeks, and turns into a dark spot that keeps getting worse.

Pour a SMALL amount of cold water on the spot (about as much as spilled). Blot with a dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. Cold only — hot water caramelizes the sugar and sets the pigment. Don't flood — water that reaches the pad causes wicking rings. You're aiming to remove sugar + fresh pigment, not deep-clean.

In a closet or under furniture, dab the Dawn + peroxide mix (step 6) on a cloth, press on the hidden carpet 2 minutes. If the cloth picks up carpet color, skip peroxide and use Dawn + cold water alone. If clean, proceed.

Mix 1 tablespoon Dawn dish soap with 1 cup 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide. Dampen a white cloth, wring out, press on the spot 5–10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes natural tannins and gradually breaks down artificial dye. Blot clean. Natural juice: 1–2 cycles. Artificial-dye kid juice: 2–3 cycles.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, bleach, OxiClean, or baking soda.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar with 1 cup cool water. Dab on a damp cloth 5 minutes. Neutralizes peroxide and keeps any remaining natural pigment in its lighter (red-acid) form rather than drifting to dark purple-blue. Blot dry. Don't skip this — it's why some people's "clean" juice spots turn blue-ish purple overnight.

Stack 6–10 white paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, leave overnight. Pulls residual sugar AND pigment out of the pad before it can wick back as a ring. This step matters extra on juice because of the sugar.

If a pale ring remains after two rounds AND the spill was an artificial-dye drink (Red 40 / Blue 1), open the blinds so sunlight falls on the spot for several hours over a day or two. UV naturally degrades synthetic food dye. Not a substitute for cleaning chemistry, but a legit finishing pass for stubborn dye ghosts.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Wool or Ring Return
homeSolutions.instruction: Call a pro if the carpet is wool, if someone already hit the spot with baking soda / OxiClean / alkaline cleaner (tannin may be locked), or if a visible ring returns after two home rounds. Tell them: "[Type] juice on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar + [sunlight]. [No alkaline used / alkaline was used before]. [Residual ring / color locked brown / artificial-dye ghost]."

steps: 1. Identify — natural juice or artificial-dye kid drink?
3. NO alkaline cleaners. DON'T skip the sugar flush.
4. Cold water small-volume flush + blot, 2–3 times.
9. Sunlight finish for stubborn artificial dye.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia was already used (tannin likely locked), if the carpet is wool and peroxide lifts color, or if a ring returns after two rounds.
warnings: NEVER rub — spreads dye, drives sugar into pad
Sugar flush is mandatory — don't skip
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never baking soda / OxiClean / alkaline cleaners on tannin
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin carpet: tolerates full protocol
Wool: pH-sensitive, peroxide risky — pro lane for tight spots
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Artificial-dye juice needs more rounds + sunlight
Sugar matrix attracts future stains if not flushed
Alkaline cleaners are the classic mistake

escalation.when: Alkaline cleaner already used, wool carpet, ring returns after two rounds, or artificial-dye ghost persists after sunlight finish.
escalation.whatToTell: "[Type] juice on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar + [sunlight]. [No alkaline used / alkaline was used]. [Residual ring / locked brown / artificial-dye ghost]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with tannin formula

contextQuestions: Natural juice (orange, apple, grape) or kid / sports drink (Gatorade, Kool-Aid, Capri Sun)?
Has anyone tried baking soda / OxiClean already?
Is there a sticky feel to the spot?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The artificial-dye fork matters — kid drinks are engineered to be colorfast. The sugar-flush step is the one most home guides skip and the reason "clean" juice spots re-collect dirt and turn dark over weeks.
